text: I am describing the procedure of laparoscopic subtotal excision of splenic cyst and upper pole partial splenectomy done for a very large splenic cyst. This was a twenty nine year old lady who had mild pain in the left flank since two weeks and her MRI and NSCT revealed that she had a very large splenic cyst in the upper pole of the spleen. They showed clear contents and the cause of this cyst was not very clear.

The surgeon uses a four-port laparoscopic technique with a supraumbilical camera and an epigastric liver retraction port. The patient is positioned semi-right lateral. An additional five-millimeter port is placed to aid in liver retraction. The gastrosplenic ligament is mobilized and divided, exposing the splenic cyst located in the upper pole.

The surgeon identifies the whitish structure of the splenic cyst located in the posterior upper pole of the spleen. Upper pole splenic vessels are seen entering the spleen, which is largely occupied by the cyst. Hemlock clip applicators are used to secure and divide these vessels. The cyst is further mobilized from the gastrosplenic ligament and splenic hilum. The splenic artery and vein are separately dissected, and the pancreatic tail is visualized and dissected away as the procedure progresses towards the upper pole.

The surgeon aspirates the cyst using a 5mm trocar and suction catheter, draining the clear fluid and collapsing the cyst wall. An assistant uses a grasper to hold the collapsed cyst, allowing further dissection of the lienorenal ligaments to separate the upper pole of the spleen from the retroperitoneum and diaphragm. The splenophrenic ligament is divided, enabling complete mobilization of the splenic cyst.

The surgeon divides the posterior layer of the linorenal ligament at the topmost attachments of the upper pole of the spleen. Superiorly, flimsy adhesions between the diaphragm and the spleen are also divided. The cyst cavity is entered, and the ligature device is used for hemostasis as the splenic parenchyma becomes visible.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to perform an upper pole partial splenectomy, excising approximately eighty percent of the large splenic cyst. A small portion of the cyst cavity extending into the middle portion of the spleen is marsupialized and left intact.

The surgeon completes the excision of the cyst along with the upper pole of the spleen. The specimen, including the upper pole and cyst, is placed in an endo bag for removal. The lower pole of the spleen is visibly preserved. A drain is inserted into the splenic fossa, and the specimen is removed through a peri-umbilical incision.
The final histology was a benign cystic lesion in the spleen.
